target.name: vibralactone
target.motivation: Vibralactone is a potent inhibitor of pancreatic lipase and features a challenging 4/5-fused bicyclic beta-lactone with an all-carbon quaternary center and two trisubstituted olefin moieties. Its unique structure and biological activity make it a promising lead compound for further investigation.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Birch reductive alkylation, intramolecular aldol reaction, late-stage lactonization
Photochemical valence isomerization, cyclopropanation, ring expansion

success_criteria.free_text: Successful construction of the bicyclic skeleton of vibralactone, particularly the all-carbon quaternary center.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond forming the all-carbon quaternary center
key_disconnections.reasoning: Inspired by the biosynthetic pathway where the quaternary center is formed late, this strategy aims to construct the challenging quaternary center in the cyclopentene ring via C-H insertion.
The beta-lactone moiety is a key structural feature and can be formed in the late stage from a beta-hydroxy acid.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond of the cyclopentene ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: To introduce the side chain and set up the stereochemistry for the beta-lactone precursor.
To form the beta-lactone ring from a simple aldehyde and acetyl chloride.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The overall strategy aimed to construct the challenging all-carbon quaternary center in the late stage, inspired by the biosynthesis. The initial plan involved forming the beta-lactone early via ketene-aldehyde cycloaddition, followed by C-H insertion. Due to issues with ketal deprotection and C-H insertion in the presence of the beta-lactone, the strategy was revised to form the five-membered ring first, followed by beta-lactone formation.

thought.background: Aldehyde 16 is readily available from fructone.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize beta-lactone 15 with the correct stereochemistry.
thought.decision: To synthesize 15, chose o-trimethylsilylquinine-catalyzed ketene–aldehyde cycloaddition with acetyl chloride, expecting to form the beta-lactone with high enantioselectivity.

outcome.observation: Beta-lactone 15 was synthesized from aldehyde 16.

evidence.text_span: Our synthetic route commenced from the known aldehyde 16 which is readily accessed in a single step from commercially available fructone [38] (Scheme 3). Following an efficient o-trimethylsilylquinine-catalyzed ketene–aldehyde cycloaddition and subsequent alkylation [36], 17 was synthesized.

thought.gap: Need to introduce the allyl group to form 17.
thought.decision: To introduce the allyl group, chose alkylation with NaHMDS, expecting to form 17.

outcome.observation: Compound 17 was synthesized.

evidence.text_span: Following an efficient o-trimethylsilylquinine-catalyzed ketene–aldehyde cycloaddition and subsequent alkylation [36], 17 was synthesized.

thought.background: Compound 17, containing the beta-lactone and ketal, is available.
thought.gap: Need to deprotect the ketal to set up for C-H insertion.
thought.decision: To deprotect the ketal, attempted standard ketal deprotection conditions, expecting to obtain the deprotected intermediate.

outcome.observation: Only decomposition of the starting material was observed upon attempted ketal deprotection.

evidence.text_span: From 17, it was envisioned that the bicyclic skeleton could be efficiently constructed through ketal deprotection followed by C–H insertion. However, when attempting to remove the ketal protecting group, only decomposition of the starting material was observed. A plausible explanation for this outcome is that the β-lactone ring, located at the β-position of the methyl ketone, may undergo facile β-elimination, although the corresponding enone product was not isolated.

thought.background: Attempted ketal deprotection of 17 led to decomposition, likely due to the instability of the beta-lactone.
thought.gap: Need a revised route that avoids early ketal deprotection in the presence of the beta-lactone or modifies the beta-lactone structure.
thought.decision: To circumvent the decomposition issue, decided to open the lactone ring of 15 and apply Fráter–Seebach alkylation to form beta-hydroxy ester 18, which would allow for ketal deprotection at a later stage.

outcome.observation: Revised synthetic route to avoid decomposition.

evidence.text_span: Facing a dead-end, the synthetic route to precursor 14 needed to be revised. From 15, after sodium methoxide-mediated ring opening of lactone, the Fráter–Seebach alkylation [39-41] was applied to afford β-hydroxy ester 18.

thought.gap: Need to open the lactone and introduce the allyl group.
thought.decision: To open the lactone and introduce the allyl group, chose sodium methoxide-mediated ring opening followed by Fráter–Seebach alkylation, expecting to form beta-hydroxy ester 18.

outcome.observation: Beta-hydroxy ester 18 was obtained. Ring opening yield: 96%, Alkylation yield: 72%.

evidence.text_span: From 15, after sodium methoxide-mediated ring opening of lactone, the Fráter–Seebach alkylation [39-41] was applied to afford β-hydroxy ester 18.

thought.gap: Need to remove the ketal protecting group.
thought.decision: To remove the ketal, chose p-TsOH in acetone/water, expecting to obtain ketone 19.

outcome.observation: Ketone 19 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: At this stage, the ketal moiety was removed and the resulting intermediate underwent Wittig olefination to yield vinyl chloride 20.

thought.gap: Need to convert the ketone to a vinyl chloride for subsequent steps.
thought.decision: To convert the ketone to a vinyl chloride, chose Wittig olefination with Ph3PCH2Cl and NaHMDS, expecting to obtain vinyl chloride 20.

outcome.observation: Vinyl chloride 20 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: At this stage, the ketal moiety was removed and the resulting intermediate underwent Wittig olefination to yield vinyl chloride 20.

thought.gap: Need to hydrolyze the ester and perform intramolecular esterification to form the beta-lactone 21.
thought.decision: To form the beta-lactone, chose hydrolysis with LiOH followed by intramolecular esterification with EDCI/HOBt/DIPEA, expecting to obtain 21.

outcome.observation: Intermediate 21 was obtained. Hydrolysis yield: 77%, Esterification yield: 46%.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent hydrolysis and intramolecular esterification furnished intermediate 21, which was then subjected to C–H insertion [42-44].

thought.gap: Need to perform C-H insertion to form the all-carbon quaternary center.
thought.decision: To form the cyclopentene ring, subjected 21 to C-H insertion conditions with KHMDS, expecting ring closure.

outcome.observation: Ring closure did not proceed, and only decomposition of 21 was observed.

evidence.text_span: To our disappointment, this ring closure still did not proceed to form the all-carbon quaternary center and only decomposition of 21 was observed. The failure is likely due to the sterically hindered environment of the substituted β-lactone ring which precludes the C–H insertion or deprotonation of the β-lactone and interrupted the generation of the alkylidene carbene.

thought.background: C-H insertion on beta-lactone 21 failed due to steric hindrance or issues with alkylidene carbene generation.
thought.gap: Need to modify the synthetic sequence to allow for successful C-H insertion.
thought.decision: To overcome the C-H insertion failure, decided to construct the five-membered ring prior to beta-lactone formation, identifying intermediate 19 as a suitable precursor for this modified approach.

outcome.observation: Modified synthetic sequence to construct the five-membered ring before beta-lactone formation.

evidence.text_span: Therefore, we modified the synthetic sequence and opted to construct the five-membered ring prior to β-lactone formation, identifying intermediate 19 as a potentially suitable precursor.

thought.background: Linear precursor 19, with a free hydroxyl group, is available.
thought.gap: Need to perform C-H insertion to form the cyclopentene ring.
thought.decision: To perform C-H insertion, treated 19 with lithiotrimethylsilyldiazomethane, expecting ring closure.

outcome.observation: Only tetrahydrofuran 22 was isolated via a formal [4+1] annulation pathway, not the desired cyclopentene.

evidence.text_span: From 19, after treatment with lithiotrimethylsilyldiazomethane [45], only tetrahydrofuran 22 was isolated via a formal [4 + 1] annulation pathway [46] (Scheme 4).

thought.background: C-H insertion on 19 (with free hydroxyl) yielded an unexpected THF product 22, suggesting the hydroxyl group interfered.
thought.gap: Need to protect the hydroxyl group to enable the desired C-H insertion.
thought.decision: To prevent interference from the hydroxyl group, protected it as the TES ether 23 and subjected it to the same C-H insertion conditions.

outcome.observation: Protected the hydroxyl group as TES ether.

evidence.text_span: Since the hydroxy group interrupted the C–H insertion, it was protected as the TES ether 23 and subjected to the same conditions.

thought.gap: Need to protect the hydroxyl group.
thought.decision: To protect the hydroxyl group, chose TESOTf and 2,6-lutidine, expecting to obtain TES ether 23.

outcome.observation: TES ether 23 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Since the hydroxy group interrupted the C–H insertion, it was protected as the TES ether 23 and subjected to the same conditions.

thought.gap: Need to perform C-H insertion to form the cyclopentene ring.
thought.decision: To perform C-H insertion, subjected 23 to lithiotrimethylsilyldiazomethane conditions, expecting ring closure.

outcome.observation: The reaction only afforded the C–Si insertion product 24, not the desired C-H insertion product.

evidence.text_span: However, the reaction only afforded the C–Si insertion product 24 [47].

thought.background: C-H insertion on both free hydroxyl and TES-protected precursors failed, yielding unexpected products or no reaction.
thought.gap: Need to find a suitable protecting group and/or substrate modification that allows for successful C-H insertion, considering the electronic requirements of alkylidene carbenes.
thought.decision: To achieve successful C-H insertion, decided to start from vinyl chloride 20, reduce it to a diol, and protect it as an acetonide 25. This modification aims to provide an electron-rich C-H bond for insertion and avoid issues with the beta-lactone or hydroxyl groups.

outcome.observation: Revised strategy to use acetonide 25 as precursor for C-H insertion.

evidence.text_span: Based on the above results, although the β-lactone was converted into the linear methyl ester 19 to decrease the potential steric hinderance associated with the fused bicyclic skeleton, substrates containing a free hydroxy group or the corresponding TES ether still failed to close the cyclopentene ring. In this scenario, it was necessary to explore additional protecting groups for the hydroxy functionality. Furthermore, given that alkylidene carbenes are electron-deficient and highly electrophilic, electron-rich C–H bonds are more prone to undergo C–H insertion [48]. Following this analysis, commencing from 20, after reduction, the 1,3-diol intermediate was transformed into acetonide 25 (Scheme 5).

thought.gap: Need to prepare acetonide 25 for C-H insertion.
thought.decision: To prepare acetonide 25, chose reduction of the ester with DIBAL-H followed by acetonide formation with DMOP and PPTS, expecting to obtain 25.

outcome.observation: Acetonide 25 was successfully synthesized.

evidence.text_span: Following this analysis, commencing from 20, after reduction, the 1,3-diol intermediate was transformed into acetonide 25 (Scheme 5).

thought.background: Acetonide 25 is available, providing an electron-rich C-H bond.
thought.gap: Need to perform intramolecular alkylidene carbene C-H insertion to form the five-membered ring.
thought.decision: To construct the five-membered ring, subjected 25 to conditions for in situ generated alkylidene carbene C-H insertion using KHMDS, expecting successful ring closure to 27.

outcome.observation: The desired five-membered ring 27 was successfully constructed via C-H insertion.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, the desired five-membered ring was successfully constructed through the in situgenerated alkylidene carbene 26 followed by C–H insertion; herein lies a significant electronic effect influencing this crucial step.

thought.background: Key intermediate 27, with the bicyclic skeleton, is in hand.
thought.gap: Need to deprotect the acetonide to reveal the diol.
thought.decision: To deprotect the acetonide, chose p-TsOH in acetone/water, expecting to obtain diol 28.

outcome.observation: Diol 28 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: With this key intermediate in hand, β-hydroxy acid 29 was synthesized through deprotection, IBX oxidation, and Pinnick–Lindgren–Kraus oxidation and the β-lactone 13 was subsequently obtained through activation of the carboxylic acid.

thought.gap: Need to oxidize the diol to the beta-hydroxy acid 29.
thought.decision: To synthesize beta-hydroxy acid 29, chose IBX oxidation followed by Pinnick–Lindgren–Kraus oxidation, expecting to obtain 29.

outcome.observation: Beta-hydroxy acid 29 was synthesized.

evidence.text_span: With this key intermediate in hand, β-hydroxy acid 29 was synthesized through deprotection, IBX oxidation, and Pinnick–Lindgren–Kraus oxidation and the β-lactone 13 was subsequently obtained through activation of the carboxylic acid.

thought.gap: Need to form the beta-lactone 13.
thought.decision: To form the beta-lactone 13, chose activation of the carboxylic acid with TsCl and Et3N, expecting intramolecular lactonization.

outcome.observation: Beta-lactone 13 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: With this key intermediate in hand, β-hydroxy acid 29 was synthesized through deprotection, IBX oxidation, and Pinnick–Lindgren–Kraus oxidation and the β-lactone 13 was subsequently obtained through activation of the carboxylic acid.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield not explicitly stated for the entire sequence to 13.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total number of reported steps including failed attempts and revisions.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of explicitly reported failed attempts.

validation.significance: The study successfully developed an approach to assemble the bicyclic skeleton of vibralactone (6) utilizing an intramolecular alkylidene carbene C–H insertion as a key step, overcoming initial challenges with beta-lactone instability and C-H insertion regioselectivity. This provides a valuable precursor (13) for the synthesis of vibralactone and its congeners.

source.title: Synthetic study toward vibralactone.
source.authors: Shi L, Song J, Li Y, Li JC, Li S, Ren L, Liu ZY, Hao HD.
